subject: LU-7801 libcfs: add function level debug message filter

messages.message: Uploaded patch set 1.

Patch Set 1:

Build Started https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/37503/

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

1 code warning(s).
If you believe the warnings are incorrect, please leave a comment with your explanations as a reply to the warning.

Patch Set 1: Verified-1

Build Failed 
 
https://build.hpdd.intel.com/job/lustre-reviews/37503/ : FAILURE

Patch Set 1:

PPC64 Builder: build succesful

Patch Set 1:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 1:

(4 comments)

Is it possible to do this with existing tools like systemtap?

The feature looks useful, but it isn't clear whether the full complexity for pattern matching is needed or if that could be done by lctl in userspace reading function names from /proc/kallsyms and just a list of functions is submitted to the kernel. I think it would be difficult to get new debugging infrastructure into Lustre in the upstream kernel.

Patch Set 1:

> (4 comments)
 > 
 > Is it possible to do this with existing tools like systemtap?
Yeah, I think sytemtap and ftrace (with additional scripts) can do this, but if want to integrate with lustre's debug mechanism, maybe very complex and extra overhead. 
 > 
 > The feature looks useful, but it isn't clear whether the full
 > complexity for pattern matching is needed or if that could be done
 > by lctl in userspace reading function names from /proc/kallsyms and
 > just a list of functions is submitted to the kernel. I think it
 > would be difficult to get new debugging infrastructure into Lustre
 > in the upstream kernel.
Hmm...I was thinking probing all the function symbols from lustre related modules (similar to ftrace does in kernel), but the problem is the same as ftrace that we need to maintain them in kernel, and additional notify should be added to update then when module load/unload, seems not less complex than this implement.

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 1:

The reason I asked about SystemTap is that there are some ideas about removing the Lustre CDEBUG functionality completely and replace it with ftrace and other more standard kernel debugging tools, so it doesn't make sense to add more complexity to the existing infrastructure. 

For the static inline vs. macro, it makes sense to keep the inline function and then make a very simple wrapper that just adds __func__ as an argument.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

I'd like to support Andreas in this. I don't like an idea to use internal specific debug mechanism when external is available for free and it's already integrated with many profiling and analysis tools. It would be much more useful if we remove CDEBUG macros or replace few of them with ftrece. Kernel Dynamic Tracing mechanism is overhead free when no tracing. Current CDEBUG approach have overhead anyway.

Patch Set 1:

Oh, I see, CDEBUG is a bit ugly and always overhead (more or less), elegant/standard debug mechanism is better, any previous discussion about this topic? glad to see it.

Patch Set 1:

Hi Dmitry,
Agreed, if we can replace CDEBUG with standard debug mechanism (e.g. ftrace), profiling and analysis will be more easy, as there are many tools built on them.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

This change cannot be cherry-picked to master.
Please rebase the change locally and upload the rebased commit for review.

Patch Set 1:

I agreed that using general ftrace is better for sure. But there is currently no schedule of replacing the CDEBUG macros. My concern is that it will take years before some one eventually start working on it. And it is also possible that developers are too used to the existing tools of Lustre, or too busy at other stuffs, and the replacement never ever happens. For example, more than an year ago, I learnt that the /proc interfaces will be replaced by /sys interface in Lustre too. And that was one of the reason that I didn't release our general framework to the community which collects statistics from Lustre /proc interfaces for monitoring purposes. And I am kind of regretting it, because I didn't see any change of /proc framework at all. And if I had released the patch, at least I will get some feedbacks and maybe we are currently thinking of merging it.

Anyway, this patch could be very helpful in some bugs we are currently debuging, if it had been merged. Those problems are not easy to reproduce. And some of them can only be reproduced on production system. Thus, we can't enabe any debug level that prints many messages. And it is far from a rare condition that we feel the insufficiency of filtering log messages when debuging, since after all debug log is almost the only tool we can count on when the bug is complex.

Patch Set 1:

Actually, the /proc files have been completely removed in the upstream kernel client, and the lctl tool in master has been updated to access parameters from /sys since 2.8.0, but this has not been backported to master yet. That might still happen for 2.9.0.

As for changing CDEBUG() into ftrace tracepoints, it is true that this is not a project that anyone else is working on, but if nobody ever tries to improve the code it will never get better. Is there a reason you couldn't look into this yourself?  Possibly changing CDEBUG() into a macro that makes a tracepoint?

Patch Set 1:

Sure, I'd like to work on replacing the CDEBUG with general log of kernel when I get some time. I didn't look into detail. Do you think kernel's log is powerful enough for debuging Lustre? The existing log system of Lustre is pretty good. My concern is that the replacement will decrease the usability of the log.

Patch Set 1:

As a side note I will be starting the sysfs port now.

Patch Set 1:

Oh if you do find cycles to do proper kernel debugging support I will gladly pitch in.

Patch Set 1:

hmm, I've been looking at the general debuging tools in the kernel. Yeah, there are a lot of dynamic and static tracing/profiling/probing tools we can use when debuging. However, I can't find a tool to replace the current log system of Lustre and at the same time provide no less ability.

Let's image that there is a bug which can only reproduce on a customer's system. And as developers, sometimes we can't login the system to use dynamic tools to debug. With the current CDEBUG implementation, we usually ask the customer to set the proper bits of CDEBUG mask, and collect the logs on not only clients but also servers. And the collected logs are pretty much all the materials we can get to analyze the bug.

The printk() with log level looks like less powerful than CDEBUG with mask. My concern is that if we replace CDEBUG definition with printk() we will lose the only way to collect information to debug under the above-mentioned circumstance. Maybe extending printk() is a solution? But I guess that will need more time to be merged.

Patch Set 1:

(8 comments)

8 style warning(s).
For more details please see http://wiki.lustre.org/Lustre_Coding_Style_Guidelines

Abandoned

The original issue has been closed as "won't Fix". If this change is still needed then a new issue should be opened and this change reopened or repushed with the new LU.

parents.subject: LU-7766 lnet: Don't call roundup_pow_of_two on zero in LNetEQAlloc

commit.subject: LU-7801 libcfs: add function level debug message filter
commit.message:
LU-7801 libcfs: add function level debug message filter

Here add a function level debug message filter support, idea comes
from ftrace.
A new proc interface named "func_filter" is added, which is used to
control the function filter, it supports regular expression (only
support just simple ones), for example:
^ll_	---> front pattern, functions start with "ll_"
_end$	---> end pattern, functions end with "_end"
read	---> full pattern, functions contains "read"
and you can add/delete pattern with symbol +/- prepend, for example:
+^ll_	---> add regular "^ll_" pattern
-_end$	---> remove regular "_end$" pattern

This version is still a WIP one, any comments are welcome.

Signed-off-by: Gu Zheng <gzheng@ddn.com>
Change-Id: I3f418927a04d5a6542233583a3f771706145ac96
